concepts.name: Key Concepts & Definitions
concepts.desc: Essential definitions, frameworks, and terminology from lectures and readings

cards.back: The application of tools, materials, processes, and scientific knowledge to create solutions and solve problems.
The practical implementation of an idea that is new to the market. Goes beyond invention to include commercial application.
The creation of something entirely new. Most inventions never become innovations.
Innovation that applies technical knowledge (tools, materials, processes) to create new solutions. Examples: nanofabrication, fuel cells.
The ability to create and retain profitability above the industry average.
The path of evolution of a technology over time. Firms can get locked into a trajectory that overshoots market needs (e.g., Eli Lilly and insulin purity).
The dominant firm in a market at a given time. Examples: Nokia in mobile phones, Eli Lilly in insulin.
A firm challenging an incumbent. Examples: Apple vs Nokia, Ford vs Selden patent holders.
Shows the lifecycle of a technology's performance over R&D effort/time: slow initial progress, rapid improvement, then diminishing returns as limits are approached.

Shows the cumulative adoption of a technology across a market over time: slow start (innovators/early adopters), rapid growth (early/late majority), then plateau (laggards). Related to Rogers' adopter categories.
The competitive landscape of a market -- how many firms compete and how market share is distributed.
Value of a product increases directly with the number of users. Example: telephone -- more users means more people to call.
Value increases through complementary products and services. Example: smartphone value increases with more apps in the ecosystem.
A firm whose products increase the value of another firm's products. Example: app developers are complementors to smartphone manufacturers.
The decision by an individual customer to buy/use a new technology or product.
The aggregate rate of adoption of a new technology across an entire market. Follows an S-curve pattern.
The replacement of one technology with another. Example: smartphones replacing feature phones.
Moving from early adopters (visionaries willing to take risks) to the early majority (pragmatists who need proven solutions). The most dangerous period for a new technology. Coined by Geoffrey Moore.
A product architecture that becomes the accepted market standard. After it emerges, competition shifts from product variation to incremental improvement and process efficiency.
The period after a dominant design emerges when many firms exit the industry. On average, about 52% of firms exit during a shakeout.
Early phase of a technology lifecycle characterized by high product innovation, many competing designs, and uncertainty about the dominant design.
Later phase of a technology lifecycle after dominant design emerges. Characterized by process innovation, incremental improvements, and focus on efficiency.
Product innovation: new or improved goods/services (e.g., Ford's Model T design). Process innovation: new or improved methods of production (e.g., Ford's moving assembly line).
Radical: fundamentally new, creates new markets or transforms existing ones. Incremental: builds on existing knowledge, improves existing products/processes.
Competence-enhancing: builds on firm's existing skills and knowledge. Competence-destroying: makes existing skills/knowledge obsolete (e.g., smartphones destroyed Nokia's feature phone competence).
Changes the way components are linked together while leaving core component knowledge intact. Difficult for incumbents because it disrupts organizational routines.
Changes core component technology but leaves the overall architecture/linkages unchanged.

A process where a smaller company targets overlooked segments (low-end or new-market), gains a foothold, then moves upmarket to challenge incumbents. NOT just any breakthrough. Uber and Tesla are NOT disruptive by this definition.

The discounted value of all future cash flows of a project minus the initial investment. Limitation: assumes certainty about future cash flows, may undervalue high-uncertainty/high-potential projects.
The discount rate that makes NPV equal to zero. Represents the expected rate of return on a project.
The minimum rate of return on a project or investment required for it to be accepted. Projects must exceed the hurdle rate.
The right, but not the obligation, to make a future investment. Valuable when uncertainty is high because it allows staged decision-making and captures the value of flexibility better than NPV alone.
Inventors/entrepreneurs know more about their innovations than investors (information asymmetry). One of the key financing frictions for innovation.
Inventors/entrepreneurs taking excessive risk with investor money without bearing the full consequences. A key financing friction alongside adverse selection.
A wealthy individual who invests directly in start-ups. Minimum ~$25,000. Earlier stage than VC, less overhead, moderate returns (<9x). Funded ventures often have 15+ angels.
Professional investor who invests in start-ups on behalf of others (LPs). Charges ~2% management fee + 20-30% carry. Demands 30-70% IRR. Subset of private equity.
Investor in a VC fund -- pension funds, university endowments, wealthy individuals. Expects 5-10x return over 7 years.
Seed: first investment, typically to build an MVP. MVP: minimum viable product, first prototype that can be sold. Series A/B/C: subsequent funding rounds to scale ('get to market').
Proportionally large ROI from a small part of a portfolio. In VC: 0.4% of investments generate 97% of returns. 63.2% of cost goes to <1x returns, while 4.3% of cost generates 61.7% of value (>10x).

The degree to which a firm can capture the financial returns (rents) from its innovation. Depends on nature of technology and strength of legal protection. Higher when knowledge is tacit or socially complex.
Knowledge that cannot be easily codified or written down. Harder for rivals to copy. Example: craft skills, organizational routines.
Knowledge embedded in relationships, culture, and organizational processes. Difficult for competitors to replicate even if they understand it.
Government-granted monopoly preventing others from exploiting an invention for 20 years (utility). Trade-off: monopoly in return for disclosure. Must be useful, novel, and non-obvious.
Non-legal protection mechanism with potentially indefinite duration. Best for processes, inputs, materials. Examples: Coca-Cola formula, Google's ranking algorithm.
Legal protection for original creative works. Granted automatically (no application needed). Duration: life of author + 70 years. Covers literary, musical, artistic works, software code.
Protection to identify the provider of a product/service. Duration: 10 years, renewable as long as the mark is in use. Must be non-descriptive and non-generic.
A dense web of overlapping patents that firms must navigate to commercialize technology. Creates barriers to entry and can stifle innovation.
Non-Practicing Entity that acquires patents without intention to use them, then sues cash-rich firms for infringement. Major problem in the US.
Control over the system architecture or platform that other firms must build upon. Source of competitive advantage (e.g., Apple's control of iOS ecosystem).
Organic: flexible, decentralized, informal, good for exploration/innovation. Mechanistic: rigid, centralized, formal, good for efficiency/exploitation.
U-form: organized by function (R&D, marketing, etc.), one P&L. M-form: organized by product/division, each with own P&L. Apple uses functional; most large firms use M-form.
Extra time/resources that allow employees to explore non-core activities. Example: Google's '20% time' that produced Gmail.
The ability to simultaneously explore new opportunities (radical innovation) and exploit existing capabilities (incremental improvement). Key organizational challenge.
ICV (Internal Corporate Venturing): in-house innovation projects. ECV (External Corporate Venturing): spin-offs, acquisitions, CVC. Modern R&D structure for incumbents.
Creating a legally separated and autonomous 'daughter' firm from a larger parent company. A form of external corporate venturing.
When established corporations invest in external startups, similar to traditional VC but with strategic (not just financial) objectives.
The process by which new innovations destroy the value of existing technologies and firms. Entrepreneurs drive this cycle, earning temporary 'Schumpeterian rents'.
Temporary above-normal profits earned by innovators before competitors catch up. Driven by creative destruction.
True uncertainty where outcomes and probabilities are unknown (as opposed to risk, where probabilities can be estimated). Common in radical innovation.
Feasibility = technically possible. Viability = commercially sustainable. Technical feasibility does NOT imply commercial viability. Examples: Concorde (feasible, not viable), metaverse (debatable).
Mathematical model of new product adoption/diffusion. Combines external influence (advertising, p-coefficient) and internal influence (word-of-mouth, q-coefficient) to predict adoption curves.
Targeting a specific, small market segment first to gain a foothold before expanding to broader markets. Related to crossing the chasm.
Horizontal: target many different market segments with the same product. Vertical: focus deeply on one specific industry or segment.

Acquiring innovations from outside the firm while leveraging internal R&D for development. P&G's C&D model: 50% external goal, 70 technology entrepreneurs, R&D cost dropped from 4.8% to 3.4% of sales.
A firm's ability to recognize, assimilate, and apply external knowledge. Required for open innovation models like C&D — without strong internal R&D to absorb external ideas, open innovation fails.

A technology with (1) pervasive use across many sectors, (2) inherent potential for continuous improvement, and (3) innovational complementarities — it spawns further innovations. Examples: electricity, steam power, internet.

Apple's concept where leaders are accountable for project success even though they do not control all teams involved. 'Good mess' = shared purpose; 'bad mess' = teams push own agendas. Enables cross-functional collaboration.

A stream of radical innovations concentrated in one industry or contiguous industries. Self-reinforcing, non-linear, difficult to predict. More frequent since the Industrial Revolution due to institutional support. Example: biotechnology cascade from DNA (1953) onward.

When a firm's product performance exceeds what the mainstream market needs. Leads to a shift in the basis of competition (e.g., from performance to convenience or price). Example: Eli Lilly's Humulin overshooting insulin purity needs.

Measures whether a paper DISRUPTS or CONSOLIDATES. High CD = disruptive (citing papers stop citing predecessors). Low CD = consolidating (citing papers also cite predecessors). Nature 2023 found proportion of high-CD papers declining.

Past choices constrain future options. Technologies, routines, and institutions persist even when alternatives exist. Related to lock-in. Example: Eli Lilly's corporate identity built around purity made it hard to shift to convenience.

Using a feature for a function other than what it originally evolved for. 'Exaptive bootstrapping' — agents solving local problems can inadvertently launch an innovation cascade. Example: Gutenberg's printing press adapted wine press technology.

cases.name: Cases & Articles
cases.desc: Key facts, arguments, and examples from Nokia, Moderna, P&G, Eli Lilly, Christensen, and more

cards.front: Nokia: What was their market position at peak and how did it decline?
cards.back: Market cap $250B (2000) to $43B (2011). In 2007 Nokia sold 437 million units and had ~35% global market share. Used Symbian OS. Failed to transition to smartphone paradigm after iPhone launched in 2007.

cards.front: Nokia: What was the Nokia 7710 and why did it fail?
cards.back: Nokia 7710 (2004) was a touchscreen smartphone -- launched 3 years BEFORE the iPhone. It failed due to poor software/UI, resistive touchscreen, and lack of app ecosystem. Nokia had the hardware vision but not the software execution.

cards.front: Nokia: How did the N95 compare to the iPhone?
cards.back: N95 (2007) was Nokia's flagship -- 5MP camera, GPS, video, music. Technically superior hardware to original iPhone. N95 sold 10 million units; iPhone sold 13 million (2007-08). But iPhone's capacitive touchscreen, UI, and App Store ecosystem won.

cards.front: Nokia: What were the 5 reasons for decline (from lecture)?
cards.back: 1) Organizational inertia / resistance to change. 2) Atmosphere of fear (middle management afraid to report bad news). 3) Complex matrixed organization slowed decision-making. 4) Over-focus on hardware, underinvestment in software. 5) Failure to recognize smartphone as new technology trajectory.

cards.front: Nokia: What were Stephen Elop's 3 strategic options?
cards.back: 1) Double down on Symbian/MeeGo. 2) Diversify by adopting Android. 3) Partner with/sell to Microsoft (Windows Phone). Elop chose option 3, eventually selling Nokia's phone division to Microsoft in 2013.

cards.front: Nokia: 'Why good companies go bad' -- what are the 6 reasons?
cards.back: 1) Strategic frames become blinders. 2) Processes harden into routines. 3) Relationships become shackles. 4) Values harden into dogmas. 5) Organizational structure resists change. 6) Resources become millstones (legacy assets/investments hold you back).

cards.front: Moderna: When was it founded and where?
cards.back: Founded in 2010 in Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA. A digital biotech company focused on mRNA technology.

cards.front: Moderna: Explain the mRNA platform analogy
cards.back: Moderna described vaccines as software: 'Hardware' = lipid nanoparticles (LNP, delivery mechanism). 'Operating System' = mRNA (the instruction code). 'App' = specific vaccine (e.g., COVID-19). The platform is programmable and enables rapid development.

cards.front: Moderna: How fast was the COVID vaccine developed?
cards.back: Vaccine DESIGNED in 2 DAYS after genetic sequence was posted (Jan 11, 2020). First batch produced in 25 days (Feb 7). Shipped to NIH in 42 days. Phase 1 trial started March 23. Unprecedented speed enabled by mRNA platform.

cards.front: Moderna: What was their funding history?
cards.back: Raised $5.1 billion pre-IPO over a decade. IPO valued at $7.5 billion. Received $955 million from BARDA (US government). Had spent a decade on mRNA R&D before COVID.
$108M (2016), $206M (2017), $135M (2018), $60M (2019), $803M (2020), $17.6B (2021). Massive spike from COVID vaccine -- nearly 300x revenue increase from 2019 to 2021.

cards.front: Moderna: What was their track record before COVID?
cards.back: Had developed 9 vaccines before COVID-19 but NONE had been brought to market. The company was 'born digital' -- integrated automation, cloud computing, and AI throughout operations. Achieved vertical integration in 2018 ($125M manufacturing plant).

cards.front: P&G Connect & Develop: What does 'Connect' and 'Develop' mean?
cards.back: Connect = find innovations from external sources (universities, suppliers, small firms). Develop = use internal R&D capabilities to bring them to market. NOT replacing internal R&D, but leveraging it with external ideas.

R&D cost dropped from 4.8% to 3.4% of sales. Goal: 50% of innovations from outside. Had 7,500 researchers + 70 technology entrepreneurs worldwide. Innovation success rate more than doubled. R&D productivity up ~60%.

cards.front: Eli Lilly: What was the insulin purity trajectory?
cards.back: Purity improved over decades: 50,000 ppm (1925) -> 10,000 ppm (1950) -> 10 ppm (1980) -> 0 ppm with Humulin (genetically engineered human insulin). Lilly invested ~$1 billion. But the market was already satisfied with animal insulin purity.

cards.front: Eli Lilly: Why did Humulin underperform commercially?
cards.back: Humulin was priced at 25% premium over animal insulin. Market response was tepid -- could not sustain the premium. The market was 'not terribly dissatisfied with pork insulin.' Lilly overshot market needs on the purity dimension.

cards.front: Eli Lilly: What was the marketers' miscalculation?
cards.back: Lilly surveyed physicians and put GREATEST WEIGHT on endocrinologists (specialists). But endocrinologists wrote only ~20% of prescriptions. GPs wrote ~80%. GPs and patients were satisfied with existing purity. Lilly listened to the most demanding customers, not the mainstream.

cards.front: Eli Lilly: How did Novo Nordisk win?
cards.back: Novo Nordisk competed on CONVENIENCE instead of purity. Their insulin pen reduced injection time from 1-2 minutes to under 10 seconds. The pen commanded a 30% price premium per unit -- easily sustained. The basis of competition shifted from purity to delivery method.

Selden (patent attorney) filed first US automobile patent in 1879 but delayed issuance until 1895 for maximum protection. Investors bought his patent rights and sued automakers. Ford was denied a license (1903) but made cars anyway and invented the assembly line. Selden won initially, but Ford won on appeal in 1911 because he used a different engine type.

cards.front: AutoStore vs Ocado: Why is this case significant?
cards.back: Same pattern as Selden vs Ford -- broad patents being used to block competition. Shows that patent strategy and patent disputes remain relevant 100+ years later.

NO. Uber did NOT originate in a low-end or new-market foothold. It launched in San Francisco (well-served taxi market), targeted existing taxi users, and offered a BETTER service (not inferior). It built its position in the mainstream FIRST -- the opposite direction of disruption. Uber is a sustaining innovation.

NO. Tesla's foothold is in the HIGH END of the auto market ($70,000+ cars). This segment is NOT uninteresting to incumbents. It has elicited significant competitive response. Tesla started premium and moved down -- opposite of disruptive path.

NUANCED. Launched in 2007 as a SUSTAINING innovation in the smartphone market (targeted same customers, won on product superiority). But its subsequent growth was DISRUPTIVE -- not of other smartphones, but of the LAPTOP as primary internet access point, through the App Store ecosystem.

cards.front: Christensen: What percentage of sustaining entrants succeed?
cards.back: Only 6% of sustaining entrants succeeded (from Christensen's disk drive study). Disruption theory: entrants should target overlooked niches, improve, then move into mainstream -- not attack incumbents head-on.

cards.front: Christensen: How does disruptive innovation work?
cards.back: 1) Incumbents focus on most profitable customers. 2) They exceed needs of some segments, ignore others. 3) Entrants target overlooked segments with 'good enough' product. 4) Entrants gain foothold. 5) Incumbents don't respond vigorously. 6) Entrants move upmarket. 7) When mainstream adopts entrant's offering, disruption has occurred.

Term from Snow Crash (1992) by Neal Stephenson. Also Ready Player One (2011) by Ernest Cline. Metaverse boom 2021-23 (Facebook renamed to Meta, October 2021). Then 'bust' in 2023 as companies pivoted to AI.

cards.front: Metaverse: What is the key lesson?
cards.back: Technical feasibility does NOT equal commercial viability. The Concorde is the classic example: technically feasible (supersonic travel worked) but commercially unviable. Same question applies to metaverse, flying cars, etc.

cards.front: Apple (HBR): Why does Apple use a functional structure?
cards.back: Apple uses a centralized functional (U-form) structure, unusual for a company its size. Aligns deep technical expertise with decision-making authority. Leaders own/learn/teach/delegate within their domain. P&L is across the whole organization, not by product division.

Apple's products need deep integration across hardware, software, and services. Independent divisions optimizing their own P&L would fragment this integration. The functional structure ensures experts make technical decisions, not general managers optimizing short-term profit.

cards.front: Concorde: Why is it a key exam example?
cards.back: Concorde is the textbook example of feasibility vs viability. Technically feasible (flew London-New York in 3.5 hours at Mach 2). Commercially unviable (extremely expensive, limited routes, noise restrictions, small passenger capacity, retired 2003).

Marcello Damiani (ex-CIO bioMerieux, hired 2015): 'You cannot blindly translate manual processes into digital processes. Processes must be redesigned to fit a digital environment.' This defined Moderna's 'born digital' approach vs traditional pharma's 'digitizing legacy.'

Swiss chemicals/biotech company. US factory one hour from Norwood; also Visp, Switzerland facility. Technology transfer to start production in <3 months (normally takes 2-3 years). Selected because they could produce at scale and were 'a highly digital company.'

Two grants: $483M (April 16, 2020) + $472M (July 26, 2020 for Phase 3) = $955M total. BARDA also gave J&J $456M and Sanofi $30M. Moderna's share was the largest single-company government award for COVID vaccine development.

cards.front: P&G C&D: Pringle Prints case study
cards.back: Needed to print images on crisps at high temperature/humidity. Created a 'technology brief.' Found solution: a bakery in Bologna, Italy run by a university professor who invented inkjet printing of edible images. Concept to launch in less than 1 year (vs ~2 years old model). Fraction of the cost of internal development.

cards.front: Christensen: What are the two types of disruptive footholds?
cards.back: 1) LOW-END: incumbents overshoot low-end needs, opening door for 'good enough' at lower price (steel minimills, discount retailers). 2) NEW-MARKET: disrupters turn nonconsumers into consumers (personal copiers vs Xerox, transistor radio, PC).

cards.front: Nokia: What was the atmosphere of fear?
cards.back: Middle management was afraid to report bad news to senior leadership. This created an information bubble where top executives were isolated from the reality of Nokia's competitive position. Combined with a complex matrixed organization that slowed decision-making.

cards.front: Nature 2023: Proposed solutions to declining disruption
cards.back: 1) Incentivize high-risk, high-reward research. 2) Initiatives like UK's ARIA (modeled on US DARPA, founded 1958). 3) AI could help by sifting information, summarizing cutting-edge knowledge, and identifying projects with breakthrough potential.

numbers.name: Numbers & Facts to Memorize
numbers.desc: Exact figures, durations, percentages, and statistics you need to know for the exam

cards.front: How long does a utility patent last?
cards.back: 20 years from the filing date.
Life of the author + 70 years (in the US).
No. Copyright is granted automatically when the work is put in tangible form. No application needed.

cards.front: How long does a trademark last?
cards.back: 10 years, renewable indefinitely as long as the mark is in use.

cards.front: What is the typical VC management fee?
cards.back: ~2% per year of committed capital.
20-30% of profits (the portion of profit retained by the VC firm).

cards.front: What return do LPs expect from a VC fund?
cards.back: 5-10x return over 7 years.

cards.front: What IRR do VCs typically require?
What percentage of VC investments generate 97% of returns?
cards.back: Only 0.4% of VC investments are responsible for 97% of returns for the entire asset class.

cards.front: What percentage of VC portfolio cost goes to <1x returns?
cards.back: 63.2% of cost goes to investments returning less than 1x (failures).

cards.front: What percentage of VC cost goes to >10x investments, and how much value do they generate?
cards.back: 4.3% of cost goes to >10x investments, but they generate 61.7% of total value.

cards.front: Of 4,000 firms seeking VC funding, how many succeed?
cards.back: Only 200 raise money. Of those, only 15 will reach NOK 1 billion in revenue.

cards.front: What is the typical minimum angel investment?
What percentage of firms exit during a shakeout?
cards.back: On average, approximately 52% of firms exit.

cards.front: What percentage of new products depend on academic research?
cards.back: 11% of new products depend on academic research.

cards.front: What percentage of new industrial processes depend on academic research?
cards.back: 9% of new industrial processes depend on academic research.

cards.front: What percentage of patented inventions lead to commercial products?
cards.back: Less than 5% of patented inventions lead to commercial products.

cards.front: What is the top motivation for patenting?
cards.back: Prevent copying -- cited by 96% of firms.

cards.front: In which industry are patents most effective? Least effective?
cards.back: Most effective: Drugs (6.5/7). Least effective: Computers (3.4/7). More effective in biological/chemical; less in mechanical/electrical.

cards.front: P&G R&D cost as percentage of sales?
cards.back: Dropped from 4.8% (2000) to 3.4% (2006) after implementing Connect & Develop.

cards.front: How fast was Moderna's COVID vaccine designed and shipped?
cards.back: Designed in 2 days after genetic sequence posted. First batch in 25 days. Shipped to NIH in 42 days.

cards.front: When was Moderna founded, and how much did they raise pre-IPO?
cards.back: Founded in 2010. Raised $5.1 billion pre-IPO over a decade.

cards.front: Nokia unit sales in 2007: total, N95, and iPhone comparison?
cards.back: Nokia sold 437 million units in 2007. N95 sold 10 million. iPhone sold 13 million (2007-08). Despite selling fewer total smartphones, Apple's ecosystem approach won long-term.

cards.front: P&G C&D: What is the external idea success rate?
cards.back: For every 100 ideas found outside P&G, only 1 ends up in the market. Extremely selective screening process.

cards.front: Eli Lilly: Insulin purity trajectory (all four data points)?
cards.back: 50,000 ppm (1925) -> 10,000 ppm (1950) -> 10 ppm (1980) -> 0 ppm (Humulin). Novo Nordisk pen: 30% premium sustained. Humulin 25% premium NOT sustained.

cards.front: Moderna: Previous fastest vaccine candidate timeline?
cards.back: Previous record: ~20 months for SARS. Moderna's COVID candidate shipped to NIH in 42 days = approximately 90% reduction in development time.

cards.front: Smil (WSJ): Battery energy density growth rate?
cards.back: 25 Wh/kg (1900) to ~300 Wh/kg (2022) = only 12x increase over 122 years = about 2%/year. Compare: microchips increased 10 million+ times since 1971 (~35%/year).

cards.front: Apple HBR: Employee and VP growth ratios?
cards.back: Employees grew 8x (17,000 to 137,000). VPs only doubled (~50 to ~96). Disciplined about limiting senior positions. Revenue grew nearly 40x ($7B to $260B) from 1997 to 2019.

thinkers.name: Tenkere & Modeller — Hvem sa hva?
thinkers.desc: Koble riktig teori til riktig person. Den viktigste decken for eksamen.

cards.back:
Hvem DRIVER innovasjon i økonomien? Startups eller store firmaer?

• Creative DESTRUCTION = startups dreper gamle firmaer (Kodak, Nokia)
• Creative ACCUMULATION = store firmaer blir enda sterkere (Intel, pharma)
• Schumpeterian RENTS = midlertidige profitter mellom innovasjon og imitasjon

cards.front: ABERNATHY & UTTERBACK — Hva handler det om?
cards.back:
Industrier går gjennom FASER:

• Fluid phase → mange firmaer, produktinnovasjon, eksperimentering
• Dominant design → industrien konvergerer på én standard
• Specific phase → prosessinnovasjon, skala, effektivitet
• Shakeout → ~52% av firmaer forsvinner

Nye firmaer best i fluid phase, incumbents best i specific phase.

Kan du BRUKE det du allerede kan når ny tech kommer?

• Competence-ENHANCING = ny tech bygger på eksisterende kompetanse → incumbent overlever (GE: X-ray→CT→MRI)
• Competence-DESTROYING = ny tech gjør kompetansen verdiløs → incumbent feiler (vakuumrør→integrerte kretser)

cards.front: HENDERSON & CLARK — Hva handler det om?
cards.back:
Endres DELENE eller KOBLINGENE?

• Incremental = begge like
• Modular = nye komponenter, samme koblinger (analog→digital telefon)
• Architectural = samme komponenter, nye koblinger (desktop→portabel DVD)
• Radical = begge nye

Architectural er FARLIGST fordi incumbenten undervurderer det — org.struktur speiler produktarkitektur.

Kundene dine LYVER om fremtiden!

• Disruptive innovation = starter i nisje, forbedrer, tar mainstream
• Incumbents feiler fordi de LYTTER til mainstream-kunder som sier 'nei'
• Entrants tar uattraktive nisjer og klatrer OPP
• Eksempler: minimills (rebar→sheet steel), workstations→minicomputers
• Uber er IKKE disruptiv (startet i mainstream)
• Tesla er IKKE disruptiv (startet i premium)

Services innoverer BAKLENGS sammenlignet med manufacturing:

1. Først: effektivisering (forsikring digitaliserer poliser)
2. Deretter: kvalitetsforbedring (bedre skadebehandling)
3. Til slutt: helt nye tjenester (online pristilbud)

I manufacturing: radical først, inkrementell til slutt
I services: inkrementell først, radical til slutt

Hvem får PENGENE — innovatøren eller imitatoren?

To faktorer avgjør:
1. Appropriability regime (sterk/svak beskyttelse)
2. Complementary assets (generic/specialized)

• EMI oppfant CT-scanner, men GE tok profitten (GE hadde sykehusnettverk)
• Moderna bygde EGNE complementary assets → beholdt verdien
• BioNTech trengte Pfizer → delte verdien

Du sitter FAST i din egen sti.

• Path dependency = tidligere valg låser deg inn
• Lock-in = kan ikke bryte ut av teknologisk sti
• Standard engineering = inkrementell forbedring langs eksisterende trajektorie
• Innovation cascades = kjeder av relaterte innovasjoner (DNA→rDNA→PCR→genom)
• Exaptation = teknologi brukt til uventet formål (Viagra: angina→erektil dysfunksjon)

5 adopter-kategorier:

• Innovators (2.5%) — elsker nyhet, risikotolerante
• Early adopters (13.5%) — validerer produktet
• Early majority (34%) — pragmatiske, vil ha komplett løsning
• Late majority (34%) — skeptiske, prissensitive
• Laggards (16%) — adopterer kun når alternativet forsvinner

Bell-kurve fordeling → S-kurve kumulativ adopsjon

cards.front: GEOFFREY MOORE — Hva handler det om?
cards.back:
Crossing the Chasm:

• Gapet mellom early adopters og early majority
• Early adopters kjøper TEKNOLOGI
• Early majority kjøper LØSNINGER
• For å krysse: vertikal marketing, beachhead strategy, komplett produkt
• Nokia N800 feilet her — ufullstendig produkt for mainstream

Bass diffusion model:

• Forecaster adopsjon basert på:
  - p = innovasjonskoeffisient (uavhengige adoptere)
  - q = imitasjonskoeffisient (påvirket av andre)
  - M = total markedsstørrelse
• Mange innovatorer + få imitatorer = konveks kurve
• Få innovatorer + mange imitatorer = S-kurve

cards.front: SCHUMPETER: Creative Destruction vs Creative Accumulation
cards.back:
DESTRUCTION: Nye firmaer ødelegger gamle (Mark I)
• Kodak, Nokia, Blockbuster dør
• Startups = motoren i økonomien

ACCUMULATION: Store firmaer akkumulerer kunnskap (Mark II)
• Intel, pharma, Boeing
• Høye barrierer hindrer nye firmaer

NB: IKKE det samme som competence-destroying/enhancing (det er Tushman!)

cards.front: TUSHMAN vs CHRISTENSEN — Hva er forskjellen?
cards.back:
Begge forklarer incumbent-feil, men ULIKT:

TUSHMAN: Feiler fordi KOMPETANSEN blir verdiløs
• Fokus: hva skjer med kunnskapen din?
• Vakuumrør-folk kan ikke lage chips

CHRISTENSEN: Feiler fordi KUNDENE avviser det nye
• Fokus: hva sier markedet?
• Nokias kunder sa 'vi vil ha bedre hardware'

Tushman = firma-perspektiv
Christensen = kunde/marked-perspektiv

Komponenter LIKE    Komponenter NYE
Koblinger LIKE:   Incremental          Modular
Koblinger NYE:    ARCHITECTURAL        Radical

• Modular: digital telefon (nye deler, samme design)
• Architectural: portabel DVD (samme deler, ny design)
• Architectural er FARLIGST fordi organisasjonen speiler produktarkitekturen

cards.front: TEECE: EMI vs Moderna — Hvorfor ulike utfall?
cards.back:
EMI (CT-scanner):
• Svak appropriability + manglet complementary assets
• GE hadde salgsteam, serviceapparat, sykehusnettverk
→ IMITATOR (GE) vant

Moderna (mRNA-vaksine):
• Sterk appropriability + BYGDE EGNE complementary assets ($125M fabrikk)
• Vertikal integrasjon, 'born digital'
→ INNOVATØR vant

Lærdommen: Innovasjon uten complementary assets = innovere for andre.

Den felles produktarkitekturen en industri konvergerer på.

• Ikke nødvendigvis teknisk BEST — men den markedet VELGER
• Eks: forbrenningsmotoren slo el-biler rundt 1900 (bedre for touring)
• Etter dominant design: konkurranse skifter fra produkt→prosess
• Shakeout: ~52% av firmaer dør
• Adopsjon tar av ETTER dominant design (kunder trenger klare metrics)

Graden av hvor godt et firma kan fange de finansielle avkastningene fra sin innovasjon.

Legal: patenter, secrecy, copyright, trademarks
Non-legal: lead time, learning curve, skala, arkitektonisk kontroll, reputasjon, kontroll over nøkkelressurser

Forsknings viser: non-legal er ofte MER effektive enn legal (unntatt pharma).

cards.front: S-CURVE: Technology vs Adoption — Hva er forskjellen?
cards.back:
TECHNOLOGY S-curve:
• Y-akse: Performance
• X-akse: Effort (R&D spending)
• Viser: hvor moden teknologien er

ADOPTION S-curve:
• Y-akse: Kumulativ adopsjon
• X-akse: Tid
• Viser: hvor mange som har adoptert

Samme form, HELT forskjellig betydning.
E-bøker: høy technology S-curve, lav adoption S-curve.

cards.front: 6 grunner til at INCUMBENTS ikke skifter S-kurve
cards.back:
1. Ingen insentiv — ny tech starter dårligere
2. Sunk investments — må skrive av gammel tech
3. Kannibalisering — ny tech tar egne salg
4. Managere ser ikke trusselen — filtrerer informasjon
5. Kan forbedre gammel tech — kjøper tid
6. Organisatorisk motstand — politisk kamp om makt

Kodak oppfant digitalkamera i 1976 men kommersialiserte ikke.
AT&T oppfant VOIP-kompresjon men var trege.

Frank Knight skilte mellom:

• RISK = du kjenner mulige utfall OG sannsynligheter (kan beregnes)
• KNIGHTIAN UNCERTAINTY = du kjenner VERKEN utfall eller sannsynligheter

Innovasjon opererer under Knightian uncertainty.
Dette er hvorfor vanlig risikovurdering (NPV) ikke fungerer godt for innovasjonsprosjekter.

NB: Det er IKKE en 'financing friction' — det er adverse selection og moral hazard som er frictions.
